turns.content: I'm trying to build a script that automates log rotation by writing directly to files, but I keep getting errors when my custom output wrapper doesn't seem to work with the write macro. It looks like it expects something specific from my type rather than just being able to store data.

The issue is likely that your custom sink isn't implementing the Write trait which is required by the internal implementation of the write! macro. The macro expands into a call to impl_write, so any type you pass must satisfy the bounds on that trait including flush and lock interfaces. You need to ensure your struct has an impl_block with the correct trait implementations rather than just storing values.

I see about the Write trait now but I thought maybe I could bypass it by using a generic function instead of the macro since my output format is quite specific. Can I just write my own helper functions that do what the macro does without going through this trait system?

While you can write custom helpers, relying on them means duplicating the logic the standard library already provides and missing out on optimizations like buffering. The Write trait allows the runtime to manage a buffer internally using impl_write which reduces I/O calls significantly compared to manual flushing every time. If you want those performance benefits you should implement the trait rather than avoiding it.

Okay so if I do implement Write manually, what exactly does that require from my struct? Do I need to handle synchronization myself with mutexes or is there a simpler way since this seems like an internal thing?

You don't necessarily need manual synchronization if you use the buffered writer pattern provided by crates like io::BufWriter. That crate wraps your output type and provides its own impl_write which handles the buffering internally for you, so your custom sink just needs to implement Write without worrying about low-level locking.

That makes sense but I'm still confused why my code fails even though I tried adding the trait manually. Is there something wrong with how I structured my type or maybe the compiler is complaining because of some hidden requirement?

The error probably comes from missing either the flush or lock bounds on your impl block since Write requires those associated methods to exist for proper buffering behavior. Make sure your struct contains a field that implements these traits and expose them correctly rather than trying to wrap everything in extra layers.
